title: Feature Hierarchy Recap
concept: Feature maps store what each layer detects, pooling helps compress and stabilize those detections, and growing receptive fields let later layers combine local clues into bigger parts and objects — together forming the full CNN feature hierarchy.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which idea tells you how much of the original image one activation can effectively 'see'?
exercises.explain: Right — receptive field tells you the span of image evidence a feature can draw from.

cards.title: Three ideas, one story
cards.body: Feature maps, pooling, and receptive fields can sound like separate topics. But together they tell one clean story about how a CNN climbs from tiny local evidence to broad object understanding.

cards.title: The hierarchy recipe
cards.body: Feature maps record where a layer detected something. Pooling summarizes nearby responses, often making them smaller and a bit more stable. Growing receptive fields let deeper activations depend on wider parts of the image. Put together, those three ideas explain how a CNN moves from local edge evidence to larger parts and then to object-level meaning.

cards.title: Notes, summaries, big-picture editor
cards.body: Imagine student reporters covering a game. One group writes tiny notes about exact moments. Another group condenses nearby notes into short summaries. An editor then reads many summaries together to understand the whole match. CNN hierarchies work in a similar way: local detections, pooled summaries, then larger-scale interpretation.

cards.title: From feather edges to a whole bird
cards.body: Unit 99's idea of feature maps says early layers can mark where tiny feather edges or beak-like lines appear. Unit 105's pooling idea says nearby strong responses can be summarized so the exact pixel position matters a bit less. Unit 107's receptive-field idea says deeper layers now 'see' a wider region, so they can combine wing, beak, and body-part clues into a bird-level feature.

cards.title: These are not isolated tricks
cards.body: It is tempting to treat feature maps, pooling, and receptive fields as three unrelated vocabulary words. They are really three views of the same machine: what was detected, how it was compressed or stabilized, and how much context the detector could use.

cards.title: The Full Hierarchy Story
cards.body: How the pieces fit from shallow to deep:

left.text:
Feature maps mark
small local detections
with precise positions

right.text:
Pooling + larger receptive fields
support bigger, steadier features
for parts and objects

options.text: Feature maps = where detections appear; pooling = nearby summarizing; receptive field = how much image a feature can use
Feature maps = file formats; pooling = optimizer; receptive field = learning rate
Feature maps = labels only; pooling = raw pixels; receptive field = hard drive space
All three terms mean exactly the same thing

exercises.explain: Right — each term names a different part of the same hierarchy story.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Larger receptive fields help deeper features combine clues from a wider region of the image.
exercises.explain: True — wider receptive fields are one reason later layers can build object-part and object-level features.

exercises.sentence: Pooling often makes nearby detections more compact and a bit more ___ to tiny shifts.
exercises.explain: Stable is right — pooling can reduce sensitivity to tiny position changes while summarizing local evidence.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best ties Units 99, 105, and 107 into one hierarchy idea?

options.text: Feature maps show detections, pooling condenses them, and larger receptive fields let deeper layers combine them into bigger concepts
Feature maps, pooling, and receptive fields are unrelated terms that never interact in a real CNN
Only receptive fields matter; feature maps and pooling do not affect hierarchy at all

exercises.explain: Exactly — those units fit together as one ladder from local detections to broad visual meaning.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'I get feature maps, pooling, and receptive fields separately, but I don't see the big picture.' Which reply best gives the full hierarchy story — and points naturally to skip connections next?

options.text: Feature maps track what each layer found, pooling makes nearby clues smaller and steadier, and growing receptive fields let deeper layers combine more context into parts and objects — which raises the next question: how can a deep network keep useful early clues available too?
They are just three separate facts to memorize, and none of them affect how a whole CNN architecture works
Pooling and receptive fields replace feature maps completely, so early layers stop mattering in deep networks
The big picture is that deeper networks should delete all earlier information as fast as possible

exercises.explain: Exactly right. A CNN hierarchy grows from local detections to broader meaning by stacking feature maps, pooling, and larger receptive fields — and that naturally leads to the next architecture question: if early clues still matter, how can later layers keep a direct path to them? That's where skip connections enter.
